report: No overt seizure activity is observed. The patient lies supine with minimal arm movements, including brief right arm extension and hand gestures, and occasional head turning. No tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms are evident.

Initial subtle head turning to the right with right arm extension; progresses to rhythmic, asynchronous jerking of the right arm and hand, with occasional upward arm movement and facial grimacing. No generalized tonic–clonic activity observed.
Blank stare with upward eye deviation; right arm flexion followed by bilateral arm flexion and rhythmic jerking of limbs. No head version or automatisms observed.

Blank stare with upward eye deviation; right arm extends and left arm flexes (fencer posture), followed by bilateral arm extension and rhythmic jerking. Mouth opens and closes rhythmically during the event.

Blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by head tilt to the left and subtle left arm flexion; gradual progression to generalized tonic stiffening with bilateral limb rigidity and rhythmic clonic jerks.

No overt seizure activity observed. Patient remains alert, with eye movements and facial expressions consistent with wakefulness. Brief hand movements occur while interacting with a laptop, including opening the device, typing, and pointing, without signs of automatisms or motor seizures.
Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks.
Blank stare with subtle rightward head version; followed by tonic stiffening of the left arm and then bilateral tonic–clonic movements.
Blank stare with subtle lip smacking; right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture) develop, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks in both limbs.
Blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture); progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks of limbs and head.

No observable seizure activity initially; patient remains motionless with eyes closed, no facial automatisms, no limb movements, and no tonic or clonic phases noted. Subsequently, an episode begins with a blank stare and upward eye deviation, followed by tonic stiffening of the body and arms, progressing to bilateral clonic jerking with asynchronous limb movements.

Initial blank stare with upward gaze deviation, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic jerking and asynchronous clonic movements involving both upper limbs and trunk, culminating in sustained posturing with open mouth and tongue protrusion.

Initial blank stare with upward gaze deviation, followed by sudden head tilt to the right with right arm extension and left arm flexion. Generalized tonic stiffening ensues, with bilateral arm flexion and extension, mouth opening widely, and tongue protrusion during the tonic phase. Rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs occur, with head and eyes deviated upward; no automatisms observed. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, and rhythmic jerking persists for several seconds.

Blank stare followed by upward eye deviation, bilateral tonic stiffening of the arms and legs, and arm elevation with flexion; progresses to rhythmic clonic jerking of all limbs and trunk, with occasional head turning to the right. Mouth opens widely with tongue protrusion and frothing at the lips.

Initial blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks involving arms and trunk, then progresses to asynchronous limb movements and vocalization.

Blank stare accompanied by head version (left or right) and corresponding arm movements—either left arm flexion with right arm extension (fencer posture) or right arm extension—progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs. Automatisms such as lip smacking are observed during some episodes. Eye deviation occurs briefly, and shaking is consistently rhythmic.

Initial blank stare with head version (right or left) and corresponding arm posturing—right arm extension or left arm flexion—followed by bilateral tonic stiffening, then evolution into generalized rhythmic clonic jerks. Episodes progress to sustained tonic posture with unresponsiveness throughout.

Blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension; progresses to tonic stiffening of the right arm with flexion at the elbow, followed by asynchronous jerking movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Initial blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerking with asynchronous limb movements and facial grimacing.

Blank stare with subtle rightward eye and head deviation, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture). This progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerking involving the arms and facial muscles. Automatisms include lip smacking and chewing movements. Patient remains unresponsive with eyes closed throughout the episodes.

Initial tonic stiffening of the right arm with flexion at the elbow, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension; progression to bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerking involving both arms and legs, with asynchronous limb movements. Head turns initially to the right, then to the left, accompanied by mouth opening. The seizure evolves into generalized tonic–clonic activity, with the patient remaining unresponsive throughout.

Initial blank stare with head deviation to the right or left, accompanied by subtle facial twitching in some instances; progresses to tonic stiffening involving one side of the body—typically manifesting as stiffening of the left or right arm and leg—or generalized tonic extension of the limbs and trunk with arms extended overhead and head thrown back. This is followed by rhythmic clonic jerking that begins focally on one side and generalizes to bilateral, asynchronous shaking of the arms, legs, and trunk. No automatisms or focal motor signs are observed.

Initial tonic stiffening involving the right arm, neck, and upper limbs, with head turning to the right and arms raised and flexed at the elbows, followed by bilateral rhythmic clonic jerking of both arms and trunk. The seizure progresses with asynchronous clonic jerks affecting the right arm and left leg. Facial grimacing, mouth opening, and upward eye deviation are observed during the event. Respiratory effort remains labored throughout. No automatisms or sustained head version noted.

Initial head version to the right with right arm extension and left arm flexion, or right arm flexion, accompanied by facial grimacing and eye deviation to the right during tonic stiffening; one episode begins with left arm tonic extension. All events progress to bilateral tonic posturing followed by rhythmic or asynchronous clonic jerking involving both arms and legs. Head turning to the left or right is observed in different episodes, with sustained unilateral posturing preceding generalized bilateral involvement. Each seizure evolves from an initial focal onset with lateralized features to bilateral convulsive activity.

Right arm extension with hand grasping the bedrail, followed by left arm flexion across the body; transitions into right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture). Head turns slightly to the right with eyes closed. Sustained tonic posturing of the right upper limb progresses to rhythmic clonic movements in the right arm and hand, then evolves into bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks. Patient remains unresponsive throughout. No generalized convulsions or automatisms observed.

Initial blank stare with rightward head version and right arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs. Sustained posturing includes right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture), head turned to the right, and unresponsiveness. No automatisms or eye movements observed.

Blank stare with rightward head version and right arm flexion, progressing to bilateral arm movements with rhythmic jerking and hand clenching; head slightly turned to the right with eyes closed. Patient remains unresponsive throughout, with no clear facial automatisms or tonic stiffening observed.

Initial subtle head turning to the left with mild left arm flexion, progressing to tonic stiffening of the left arm and then bilateral clonic jerks; patient remains unresponsive with eyes open and irregular breathing. Right arm flexion with hand clenching followed by tonic stiffening of the right upper limb, without spread to the contralateral side or generalized clonic activity; patient remains alert with eyes open throughout, no automatisms or head version noted.

Right arm extends upward with fingers slightly curled, then gradually relaxes; left arm remains relatively still with mild flexion at the elbow. No facial automatisms or head deviation observed. Bilateral arm movements are asynchronous, with right arm movement preceding left arm elevation and return to rest.

Initial blank stare with subtle leftward head version, followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension (fencer posturing). Progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and trunk. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Blank stare with rightward head version and asymmetric arm movement—either right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posturing) or right arm extension—followed by evolution into generalized tonic stiffening and rhythmic bilateral clonic jerks involving all limbs.
Blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension; evolves to bilateral arm flexion and extension with rhythmic jerking.

Initial subtle movement of the right hand with slight flexion, progressing to tonic stiffening of the right arm and extension of the left arm (fencer posture). This evolves into rhythmic clonic jerking of both arms, with the right arm more prominent, and head turning to the right. The patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Initial subtle head turning to the right with right arm extension, progressing to blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension, followed by generalized tonic stiffening and bilateral clonic jerks; arms flexed and extended asynchronously, with eyes open and upward gaze. Rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs occur during the episode, accompanied by automatisms including lip smacking and tongue thrusting.
Initial blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension; evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs.

Initial subtle eye deviation upward or to the right, followed by asymmetric motor signs including right arm flexion or extension and left arm extension or flexion (fencer posture), with head turning to the right. Progression to bilateral rhythmic jerking involving arms and legs, evolving into generalized tonic–clonic movements of the limbs and trunk. Brief postictal stillness ensues.

No overt motor signs or seizure activity are observed; the patient remains supine with eyes closed, hands resting on the abdomen, and no visible tonic or clonic movements, head version, facial automatisms, limb movements, or postural changes throughout the recording.

Initial subtle right arm flexion and slight head turn to the right, progressing to generalized tonic stiffening with bilateral arm extension, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and trunk, culminating in sustained bilateral clonic movements; subsequently, no observable seizure activity with the patient remaining motionless, eyes closed, arms at rest, and no facial or limb movements.

Initial blank stare with subtle rightward head version, followed by tonic stiffening of the right arm and leg and head turned to the right with right arm extended. Progresses to bilateral clonic jerks with asynchronous limb movements and generalized rhythmic shaking; no automatisms observed. Patient gradually returns to supine position, remains unresponsive throughout with eyes closed.

Blank stare with upward eye deviation and subtle rightward head version; right arm flexion and elevation above head, followed by brief bilateral clonic jerks. Minimal facial movement and no vocalization observed; no further motor evolution noted.

Initial blank stare with subtle left head version, followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension (fencer posture); progression to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks of limbs and trunk.

Initial blank stare followed by leftward head version and left arm flexion with right arm extension (fencer posturing), progressing to bilateral tonic stiffening with upward extension of both arms and eyes deviated upward throughout. This evolves into bilateral clonic jerking involving all limbs and the trunk, followed by brief postictal flaccidity and unresponsiveness.

Initial blank stare with upward eye deviation and subtle mouth movements, followed by tonic stiffening of the body and arms; progression to bilateral arm flexion and extension, then rhythmic clonic jerking of both arms and legs, accompanied by head thrusting, arm flailing, mouth opening, tongue protrusion, and sustained vocalization.

Initial tonic stiffening of the right arm with extension and slight head turn to the right; progresses to bilateral arm extension with rhythmic clonic jerking, predominantly in the right upper limb. The patient remains unresponsive with eyes open and mouth slightly agape throughout.

No overt seizure activity observed. Patient remains alert and awake, reading a book throughout, with no blank stare, automatisms, or motor movements suggestive of seizure. No tonic or clonic movements noted.
No overt seizure activity observed. Patient remains awake, with intermittent hand movements near the mouth and occasional eye blinking. No tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms noted.
No observable seizure activity. Patient remains seated, alert, and responsive throughout, with no blank stare, automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or posturing.

No observable seizure activity. Patient is awake, sitting upright, writing in a notebook with both hands, and appears alert and responsive. No facial automatisms, head turning, or limb movements suggestive of seizure.

No overt seizure activity observed. Patient remains seated in bed, alert, and responsive, holding a remote control and interacting with surroundings. No facial automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic movements, or posturing noted.
Blank stare with rightward head version; right arm extends upward and remains elevated, while left arm flexes and holds a cup. No generalized tonic–clonic movements observed.
Blank stare with hands covering face, followed by head tilt and eyes closing; no overt motor activity or clonic movements observed.

Initial blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension, accompanied by left arm flexion (fencer posture), progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of both limbs. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event. Between events, the patient remains supine with eyes closed and head slightly tilted to the right, demonstrating no overt motor activity, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, facial automatisms, or limb movements.

No overt seizure activity observed. Patient is awake, seated upright, actively using a tablet with both hands, and appears alert and responsive. No facial automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or head versioning noted.
No overt seizure signs observed; patient remains seated, focused on tablet, with no facial automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic movements, or head version.

Initial blank stare with sudden bilateral arm flexion and extension, progressing to generalized tonic–clonic movements involving all limbs, head, and trunk with rhythmic jerking; patient remains unresponsive throughout.

Initial blank stare followed by right arm flexion and upward movement; progression to bilateral arm jerking with asynchronous, rhythmic clonic movements; mouth opens with tongue protrusion and brief facial twitching.
Blank stare with sudden upward eye deviation, followed by bilateral arm flexion and extension; rhythmic jerking of both limbs ensues, progressing to generalized tonic–clonic movements.

Initial blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension; progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs. Automatisms include mouth movements and hand gestures.

Initial blank stare followed by bilateral arm extension above the head with mouth opening and tongue protrusion; sustained tonic posturing evolves into rhythmic clonic jerking of both arms and upper body.

Initial tonic stiffening of both arms with hands clasped above the head, followed by asynchronous clonic jerking of the right arm and then the left; later, generalized bilateral clonic movements with head turning to the right and mouth opening.

Initial tonic stiffening of both arms with hands raised above the head, progressing to rhythmic clonic jerking of both arms and head. Mouth opens widely during the episode, with sustained posturing and no return to baseline by the end.

Generalized tonic–clonic seizure: abrupt onset with arms raised and flexed overhead, followed by rhythmic clonic jerking of both limbs; mouth open with tongue protrusion; eyes open and deviated upward.

Initial blank stare with mouth slightly open, followed by sudden bilateral arm extension above the head with fingers splayed and mouth opening; arms then flex at elbows with hands moving toward midline, maintaining tonic posture; no clonic movements observed. Patient remains awake and alert with eyes open, demonstrating voluntary hand movements and interaction with a person at the bedside. No overt seizure activity, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, automatisms, or posturing are present.

Bilateral arm extension with fingers splayed, mouth open, and eyes fixed upward; arms remain elevated and stiff throughout, with no clonic movement or lateral asymmetry. No head version or automatisms observed.

Blank stare with sudden left arm extension above head, followed by rhythmic jerking of the same arm; eyes remain open and fixed, mouth slightly agape throughout. No spread to other limbs or generalized convulsions observed.

Initial blank stare followed by sustained upward deviation of the eyes and right arm extension with hand flexion; mouth open, jaw slightly agape throughout. No clonic movements or spread to contralateral side observed.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm tonic extension with slight flexion at the elbow, evolving into bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks; head turns slightly to the right during the episode; no automatisms observed.

Initial blank stare with rightward head version and right arm flexion; evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs. Bilateral arm movements become asynchronous, with right arm flexion and left arm extension noted during the seizure. No facial automatisms or head version are observed after the initial head deviation.

Initial tonic stiffening of the body with head thrust backward, followed by bilateral arm flexion and extension; rhythmic clonic jerking of both arms and torso ensues, with eyes open and upward deviation. Patient exhibits sudden blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, evolving to bilateral tonic stiffening with rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs.

Initial blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by bilateral arm flexion and extension; progresses to generalized tonic–clonic movements with head turning to the right. This includes rightward head version and right arm extension, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic jerking with asynchronous limb movements. The mouth opens and closes rhythmically during the event.

Blank stare accompanied by subtle lip smacking, upward eye deviation, or head version (right or left) is observed, with initial focal motor signs including unilateral arm flexion or extension and clonic jerking of the ipsilateral arm and leg. In some episodes, focal onset with rightward head version and right arm extension progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening followed by generalized rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs. Leftward head version is associated with initial clonic movements in the left arm and leg, which may spread to bilateral limb involvement. Brief tonic stiffening of the trunk may precede generalized clonic activity. Rhythmic facial twitching and bilateral arm jerking occur in association with the blank stare and eye deviation. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, with no progression to bilateral tonic–clonic activity noted in every case.

Blank stare, often with upward eye deviation and subtle or overt rightward head version, is consistently observed, followed by motor manifestations including right arm flexion or extension and left arm extension (fencer posture). This progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening, then evolves into rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs, often with asymmetric intensity more pronounced on the right side. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, with eyes open and mouth slightly agape. Automatisms such as lip smacking and hand-to-face movements are noted in some episodes.

Patient remains supine with eyes closed throughout, showing no overt motor activity. There is no observable tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, or asymmetric posturing. No automatisms, head version, limb movements, or facial automatisms are noted, and respiratory pattern appears regular without apnea or gasping. Episodes characterized by blank stare are observed, some followed by rightward head version and tonic stiffening of the right arm and leg, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic clonic jerks of all limbs and asynchronous limb movements. Upward eye deviation and occasional facial grimacing occur during events. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all observed episodes.

Blank stare accompanied by mouth opening and variable eye deviation (including upward gaze) is consistently observed, followed by development of fencer posturing—manifesting as asymmetric arm positioning with either right arm flexion and left arm extension or vice versa. This posturing progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening and evolves into rhythmic clonic jerking involving both arms and legs. Additional features include rhythmic jaw movements and tongue protrusion.

Blank stare with sudden upward eye deviation or vocalization, accompanied by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posturing); progression to tonic stiffening initially in the right arm, then bilateral generalized tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks involving all limbs.

Initial blank stare with upward gaze deviation, followed by tonic stiffening of the head and neck. Bilateral arm extension with slight asymmetry, progressing to generalized clonic jerks involving the limbs and trunk. Mouth opens widely with tongue protrusion during the clonic phase.

- Initial blank stare, often with upward gaze deviation or rightward head version and corresponding asymmetric arm posturing (e.g., right arm extension with left arm flexion or fencer posture), may be accompanied by subtle lip smacking; this evolves into bilateral tonic stiffening of the arms and legs, with arms extended or flexed and legs straight, body stiffened, head tilted back, mouth open with or without vocalization, and tongue protrusion in some instances; eyes may remain open, deviated upward, or closed throughout. - Tonic phase transitions into rhythmic, symmetric clonic jerking involving all four limbs, with asynchronous movements noted in some observations; no focal automatisms, asymmetric posturing, or lateral head version observed during clonic phase in most cases. - Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event, with generalized motor seizure activity persisting as continuous bilateral clonic movements; head position is typically midline or tilted posteriorly, without consistent lateral deviation in later phases.

Patient exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle head version to the left, with left arm flexion and right arm extension (fencer posturing), progressing to bilateral rhythmic jerking of limbs; no vocalizations or eye movements are observed. Outside of this episode, no overt seizure activity is noted: the patient remains supine with eyes closed, hands resting on abdomen, without head version, limb movements, facial automatisms, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or asymmetric posturing. Occasional subtle hand motion near the abdomen is observed, but no automatisms, change in posture, or muscle tone is present. Respiratory pattern remains regular and unremarkable throughout.

No overt seizure activity is observed in multiple segments where the patient remains supine or seated with eyes open, occasionally moving the head slightly to the right or left, making minor or subtle hand movements—including slight flexion and extension of the right hand—adjusting the blanket, or shifting gaze. The patient appears alert and responsive throughout these periods, wearing glasses in one instance, with no tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, automatisms, head version, or altered consciousness noted. In other segments, a blank stare is accompanied by subtle or overt head version (rightward or leftward) and upward gaze deviation, followed by focal motor signs including unilateral arm flexion or extension—such as fencer posturing (right arm flexion with left arm extension)—which progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening and rhythmic clonic jerks involving upper and lower extremities. Some events remain unilateral with rhythmic clonic jerking of the right upper extremity without spread to bilateral involvement or automatisms, while others evolve to asymmetric or asynchronous clonic movements. During these episodes, the patient remains unresponsive. One segment notes the patient with eyes closed, minimal spontaneous movement, and no clinical seizure activity.

Patient initially exhibited a blank stare with upward eye deviation, followed by bilateral arm flexion and tonic stiffening, which progressed to rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs, accompanied by head tilt backward and mouth open. Following this event, no further overt seizure activity was observed. The patient remained supine with eyes open, occasionally moving arms and hands and making slight adjustments in position, including looking upward. Throughout the remainder of the observation, there were no additional episodes of tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, automatisms, or head version. The arms remained relaxed at the sides, with no abnormal posturing, facial automatisms, or changes in eye movement, respiration, or limb position noted.

Initial subtle head turning to the right with mild right arm flexion, followed by progressive tonic stiffening of the upper body and arms; evolves into bilateral clonic jerking with asynchronous limb movements and upward eye deviation. Tonic stiffening of the body with arms extended and hands clenched, accompanied by bilateral clonic jerking of the limbs and trunk; head tilted back, mouth open, and rhythmic jerking of arms and legs. Head remains midline in some segments, without lateral asymmetry or automatisms during clonic phase. Patient remains unresponsive throughout the event.

Patient exhibits a blank stare with rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to right arm flexion with rhythmic clonic jerking of the right upper limb; no bilateral involvement or generalized tonic–clonic activity observed. In other periods, evolution to bilateral tonic stiffening is noted, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms and legs; patient remains unresponsive throughout these episodes. During baseline intervals, patient remains supine with eyes open, head centered, and no overt motor activity; limbs are relaxed and symmetrically positioned, with regular respiratory pattern and no signs of apnea or gasping. No facial automatisms, limb movements, posturing, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or automatisms are observed during these periods. At times, patient appears awake and alert with occasional hand movements and eye blinking, while at other times, patient appears unresponsive without any motor activity. Subtle lip smacking is noted during some episodes with impaired responsiveness.

Blank stare, often accompanied by subtle facial twitching or grimacing, is consistently observed and typically followed by rightward head version with right arm extension or flexion. Variability in initial limb posturing includes right arm flexion, right arm extension, left arm extension, left arm flexion, or fencer posturing (right arm extension with left arm flexion). In most episodes, the seizure progresses to bilateral tonic stiffening of all limbs, followed by rhythmic clonic jerking involving both arms and legs. Facial grimacing and rhythmic opening and closing of the mouth are noted during some events. Jerking may occasionally present as asynchronous in the upper limbs without overt tonic–clonic activity or automatisms. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the events. One observation showed no overt seizure activity, with the patient remaining supine, eyes closed, exhibiting no facial automatisms, head version, tonic or clonic movements, and limbs relaxed and motionless.
Blank stare with subtle leftward head version; right arm extends and flexes rhythmically, followed by bilateral arm jerking and generalized clonic movements. Patient remains unresponsive throughout.

No overt seizure activity observed. Patient remains supine, alert, and responsive, with eyes open, intermittently moving limbs, adjusting head position, manipulating objects, and adjusting the blanket. No tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, head version, blank stare, automatisms, or abnormal posturing noted.

Initial blank stare with lip smacking, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension (fencer posture); evolves to bilateral tonic stiffening with arms extended and legs rigid, and head turning to the left. Tonic phase progresses to bilateral clonic jerks involving limbs and trunk, with mouth opening widely, tongue protrusion, frothing, and eyes deviating upward and remaining fixed.
